name: I got bumped by a forklift this morning and thought I was fine, but now my back is killing me. Did I wait too long to report it?
description: It is not too late, but you must report it now. Adrenaline often masks pain; medical records showing you sought treatment as soon as symptoms appeared will help

mainEntity.name: I got bumped by a forklift this morning and thought I was fine, but now my back is killing me. Did I wait too long to report it?

acceptedAnswer.text: It is not too late, but you must report it now. Adrenaline often masks pain; medical records showing you sought treatment as soon as symptoms appeared will help bridge the gap for insurance.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Internal injuries from a forklift impact, like organ damage or slow-bleeding internal trauma, aren\'t always obvious in the first hour. If you start feeling pain, dizziness, or stiffness later, you must report it immediately. The insurance company will likely try to claim that because you didn\'t complain at the scene, the injury must have happened at home that night.

The key is to seek medical attention the moment you realize something is wrong and explicitly tell the doctor, "I was involved in a forklift accident at work yesterday."

How to handle delayed symptoms:
- Update your supervisor in writing the moment symptoms appear.
- See a doctor immediately and explain the delay in symptoms.
- Document any outward signs, like bruising that developed overnight.

Be honest with the doctor about why you didn\'t feel the pain initially (like adrenaline or shock). This notation in your medical records is vital for your claim.

Lawyers specialize in connecting "delayed" symptoms to the original accident through expert medical testimony.
